nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving or still freshwater associated with the roots of floating plants such as water hyacinth, in densely vegetated marshes and partially flooded lowland habitats. The species is documented from the Tha Chin River drainage and surrounding Central/Eastern Thai lowland waterways and rice-field margins.
nativeRange.biotope: Thai lowland soft-water marsh and floating-plant root tangle

decorPreferences: dense moss carpets (Java moss, flame moss, Christmas moss) covering driftwood and rockwork
floating plants such as water hyacinth, water lettuce or frogbit to mimic the natural root-tangle habitat
gentle flow that suspends fine particulate food without buffeting the crabs
well-established biofilm and visible particulate matter in the water column
fine sand or smooth gravel substrate
tight-fitting lid (low escape risk but useful for jumping tankmates and evaporation control)

diet.notes: Predominantly a filter feeder and particulate scavenger. The hair-like setae covering the carapace, legs and claws are used to capture suspended microorganisms, biofilm and detritus directly from the water column and from plant roots. Has been documented accepting very small sinking foods such as powdered fry food, crushed flake, finely ground pellets and the dust generated by squeezing a dirty sponge filter into the current. Some specimens will also pick at biofilm on moss and decor. Tanks that are too sterile have been reported to cause slow starvation, so a mature, lightly nutrient-rich water column is generally advised over an immaculately clean one.

goodWith: Other filter-feeding shrimp such as Bamboo Shrimp
Otocinclus and other small peaceful catfish
Small peaceful nano fish (Chili Rasbora, Pygmy Cory, Endler's livebearer)
avoidWith: Bettas (risk varies; individual bettas may pick at the crabs)

imageCandidates.notes: Sean Murray, 3648x2736. Sharpest, cleanest dorsal view of a single live individual; whole animal in frame with round hairy carapace, dark eyes and long banded spider-like legs clearly visible. Best hero shot.
Sean Murray, 3648x2736. Group of three individuals, good for this shoaling species; front crab reasonably sharp showing banded legs and mottled carapace, others softer.
Sean Murray, 3648x2736. Live crab beside floating duckweed, echoing its floating-plant-root habitat; whole animal in frame with banded legs, slightly soft.

summary: The Thai Micro Crab (Limnopilos naiyanetri) is one of the smallest freshwater crabs in the hobby, reaching barely 1 cm across the body. Native to Thailand, it filter-feeds using hair-like bristles and is fully aquatic and genuinely peaceful — one of the few crabs that can be kept safely alongside dwarf shrimp.
careNotes: The Thai Micro Crab is among the smallest freshwater crabs in the hobby, with an adult carapace of roughly 1 cm and a leg span around 2.5 cm. Unlike most aquarium crabs it is fully aquatic and does not require land access; it is also genuinely peaceful and is one of the few crabs that can be safely combined with dwarf shrimp and nano-fish communities. Water conditions should be soft to moderately hard and slightly acidic to neutral — a temperature of 22 to 27°C, pH of 6.5 to 7.5, general hardness of 4 to 15 dGH, and carbonate hardness of 2 to 6 dKH cover the commonly cited range, with some sources reporting tolerance up to around pH 8.0. The species is endemic to the Tha Chin River drainage and adjacent slow-moving, vegetation-choked waterways of Central and Eastern Thailand, where it lives among the submerged roots of floating plants such as water hyacinth. The crabs are extremely shy, cryptic, and largely nocturnal — even in well-established tanks they are typically only partially visible inside moss or among root tangles. Because of this, dense moss cover, floating-plant roots and a mature biofilm layer are generally considered essential rather than decorative. A minimum tank of around 20 L (5 US gal) is widely cited; a small group of 5-6 is commonly recommended so that natural behaviour can be observed at all. The species is reportedly sensitive to ammonia and nitrite spikes typical of immature tanks and should only be added to a fully cycled, biologically mature setup. Like all decapod crustaceans, the Thai Micro Crab is sensitive to copper-based medications and should not be kept in any tank treated with them. Lifespan in captivity has been reported around 1 to 1.5 years, possibly reaching 2 years in optimal conditions; this is short relative to most aquarium shrimp. Escape risk is low — the crabs are fully aquatic and have no real climbing ability, so a sealed lid is helpful mainly for tankmates and evaporation rather than for the crabs themselves.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been reliably accomplished. Females have been reported carrying eggs and releasing larvae in aquaria, but the larvae have proven extremely difficult to rear. Unlike many filter-feeding shrimp, the larvae are reported NOT to require a brackish phase; instead they appear to need stable freshwater with strong overhead lighting (used by the larvae for orientation) and abundant micro-particulate food. To date the trade is dominated by wild-caught Thai imports. Captive-breeding attempts should be considered experimental and are not within reach of typical home-aquarium practice.

additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (~1.3 cm / 0.5 in), temperature 22-28°C, pH 6.5-8.0, hardness 6-15 dKH, lifespan ~1.5 years, peaceful/nocturnal behaviour, Tha Chin River endemism, captive breeding failure, slow molt frequency.

additional.name: Aqua Imports: Thai Micro Spider Crab
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (~0.5 in / 1.3 cm), temperature 77-82°F (25-28°C), pH 6.8-7.4, minimum tank 6 gal (~23 L), peaceful temperament, native range Central/Eastern Thailand including rice-field margins, filter-feeding plus particulate scavenging diet.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Reported to molt less frequently than typical dwarf shrimp because growth is slow and adult size is reached only after a series of molts. Post-molt individuals are soft and translucent for a short period and are particularly vulnerable; abundant moss cover and a mature tank with no aggressive tankmates is generally advised to give them safe hiding space during this window. Specific interval is not consistently reported in care sources.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Females have been observed carrying eggs and releasing larvae in tanks, but rearing the larvae through to settlement has not been reliably achieved. Unlike many filter-feeding atyid shrimp, the larvae are reported NOT to require a brackish phase, but they appear to need very specific lighting and micro-particulate food conditions that home aquarists have not consistently reproduced. Commercial supply is dominated by wild-caught Thai imports.
